summary:
Netiv Hagdud is a Pre-Pottery Neolithic A site in the lower Jordan Valley, located north of Jericho in modern-day Israel, dated to approximately 9500–8700 BCE. The site is one of a cluster of small early Neolithic villages in the Jordan Valley — alongside Gilgal, Dhra, and Jericho itself — that collectively document the earliest phase of agricultural settlement in the southern Levant.

Excavations at Netiv Hagdud have produced substantial botanical assemblages including large quantities of wild-type barley alongside early domesticated einkorn wheat, reflecting the mixed economy of a community in the early stages of plant domestication. The site also yielded evidence of intensive gazelle hunting, storage pits, and round to oval mud-brick structures characteristic of the PPNA architectural tradition. Netiv Hagdud provides comparative data for understanding how [[event:1304|PPNA communities]] across the Jordan Valley were simultaneously experimenting with cultivation while still relying heavily on hunted and gathered resources — a flexible, multi-strategy subsistence approach that characterized the earliest agricultural communities.
